concepts.name: Completeness of the Real Numbers
concepts.intuition_gloss: The number line has no pinholes — any collection of reals with a ceiling has a single lowest ceiling actually sitting on the line.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every nonempty subset of R that is bounded above has a least upper bound (supremum) in R, the axiom distinguishing R from Q.

concepts.name: Cauchy Sequence
concepts.intuition_gloss: A sequence whose terms huddle ever closer to each other — you can tell it's settling down without knowing where it's headed.
concepts.formal_gloss: A sequence (a_n) such that for every ε > 0 there exists N with |a_m - a_n| < ε for all m, n > N; in R, Cauchy is equivalent to convergent.
sources: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis Ch. 3

concepts.name: Infinite Series
concepts.intuition_gloss: Adding up infinitely many numbers one after another and asking whether the running total settles on a finite answer.
concepts.formal_gloss: The limit of the partial sums s_n = a_1 + ... + a_n of a sequence; convergence is decided by tests such as comparison, ratio, and integral tests.

resources.title: Khan Academy: Infinite sequences and series

concepts.name: Intermediate Value Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: A continuous path from below a level to above it must cross that level somewhere — you can't get across a river without touching the water.
concepts.formal_gloss: If f is continuous on [a,b] and y lies between f(a) and f(b), then f(c) = y for some c in [a,b]; a consequence of the connectedness of intervals.

concepts.name: Extreme Value Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: A continuous function on a closed, bounded stretch must actually reach a highest and a lowest point — no peak forever just out of reach.
concepts.formal_gloss: A continuous real-valued function on a compact set attains its maximum and minimum; on R, compact means closed and bounded (Heine–Borel).

concepts.name: Mean Value Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: On any smooth trip, there is an instant when your speedometer reads exactly your average speed for the whole trip.
concepts.formal_gloss: If f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), there exists c with f'(c) = (f(b) - f(a))/(b - a); the bridge from local derivative facts to global behavior.
sources: Standard calculus and real analysis curricula

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
concepts.intuition_gloss: Accumulating a quantity and measuring its rate of change are inverse operations — area under a rate curve is total change.
concepts.formal_gloss: If f is continuous on [a,b], then F(x) = ∫_a^x f(t)dt satisfies F' = f, and ∫_a^b f = G(b) - G(a) for any antiderivative G of f.

concepts.name: Power Series
concepts.intuition_gloss: An infinitely long polynomial — a recipe that builds functions like sine and exponential out of nothing but powers of x.
concepts.formal_gloss: A series of the form Σ c_n (x - a)^n, converging absolutely inside a radius of convergence R and defining an analytic function there.

concepts.name: Taylor Series
concepts.intuition_gloss: Cloning a function near a point using only its derivatives there — better and better polynomial impressions of the real thing.
concepts.formal_gloss: The series Σ f^(n)(a)(x-a)^n / n!, which represents f near a when the remainder term tends to zero.

concepts.name: Uniform Convergence
concepts.intuition_gloss: A sequence of functions closing in on a target curve everywhere at the same speed, so good properties like continuity survive the limit.
concepts.formal_gloss: f_n → f uniformly if sup_x |f_n(x) - f(x)| → 0; uniform limits preserve continuity and allow interchange of limit with integral.

concepts.name: Partial Derivative & Jacobian
concepts.intuition_gloss: The slope of a landscape measured along one compass direction at a time — and the matrix that bundles all directions into one linear snapshot.
concepts.formal_gloss: ∂f/∂x_i is the derivative of f with the other variables held fixed; a differentiable map's Jacobian matrix of partials is its best linear approximation.

concepts.name: Gradient
concepts.intuition_gloss: The arrow that points straight uphill on a landscape, with length telling you how steep the climb is.
concepts.formal_gloss: ∇f, the vector of partial derivatives of a scalar field; the direction of steepest ascent, satisfying D_v f = ∇f · v.
sources: Ubiquity in optimization/ML gives high leverage score

concepts.name: Multiple Integral
concepts.intuition_gloss: Adding up a quantity spread over an area or volume by slicing the region into tiny tiles or bricks.
concepts.formal_gloss: The integral of f over a region in R^n, computable as iterated one-variable integrals when Fubini's theorem applies, with change of variables governed by the Jacobian determinant.

concepts.name: Vector Calculus & Stokes' Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: What flows out through a boundary equals what is generated inside — the fundamental theorem of calculus scaled up to curves, surfaces, and volumes.
concepts.formal_gloss: ∫_∂M ω = ∫_M dω: the integral of a differential form over the boundary of an oriented manifold equals the integral of its exterior derivative over the interior, unifying Green's, divergence, and classical Stokes theorems.

concepts.name: Inverse & Implicit Function Theorems
concepts.intuition_gloss: If the linear snapshot of a map is invertible at a point, the map itself is locally invertible there — and equations can locally be solved for some variables in terms of the others.
concepts.formal_gloss: A C^1 map with invertible Jacobian at a point is a local diffeomorphism; correspondingly, F(x,y) = 0 with invertible ∂F/∂y locally defines y as a C^1 function of x.
sources: Prerequisite for manifold theory (Lean mathlib dependency structure)

concepts.name: Banach Fixed-Point Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Keep applying a map that always shrinks distances and everything spirals into one inevitable fixed point.
concepts.formal_gloss: A contraction on a complete metric space has a unique fixed point, obtained as the limit of iterating the map from any starting point.
sources: Powers Picard existence theorem for ODEs and inverse function theorem — high leverage

concepts.name: Asymptotics & Big-O Notation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Comparing how fast functions grow or shrink in the long run, ignoring constants and small-fry terms.
concepts.formal_gloss: f = O(g) if |f(x)| ≤ C|g(x)| for large x; the calculus of dominant behavior of functions as an argument tends to a limit.
sources: Bridges analysis to computer science and analytic number theory

concepts.name: Convexity
concepts.intuition_gloss: A bowl-shaped function — every chord lies above the graph, so any local bottom is the global bottom.
concepts.formal_gloss: f is convex if f(tx + (1-t)y) ≤ tf(x) + (1-t)f(y) for t in [0,1]; for twice-differentiable f, equivalent to f'' ≥ 0.
sources: Central to optimization and inequalities (Jensen) — high cross-territory leverage

concepts.name: Measure & Lebesgue Measure
concepts.intuition_gloss: A rigorous notion of length, area, and volume robust enough to size up wildly irregular sets — with the surprise that some sets cannot be measured at all.
concepts.formal_gloss: A countably additive nonnegative function on a σ-algebra of subsets; Lebesgue measure extends length from intervals to all Borel (and Lebesgue-measurable) subsets of R.

resources.title: Terence Tao, An Introduction to Measure Theory

concepts.name: Lebesgue Integral
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of slicing the ground under a curve into vertical strips, slice by height and ask how much ground sits at each level — an integral that survives limits gracefully.
concepts.formal_gloss: The integral built from measures of level sets of measurable functions, extending the Riemann integral and satisfying the monotone and dominated convergence theorems.

resources.title: Terence Tao, An Introduction to Measure Theory

sources: Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis Ch. 1

concepts.name: Normed & Banach Spaces
concepts.intuition_gloss: Treat entire functions as single points in an infinite-dimensional space with a notion of length, where analysis and linear algebra fuse.
concepts.formal_gloss: A vector space with a norm inducing a complete metric; the setting of functional analysis, where linear operators between such spaces are studied.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.102 Introduction to Functional Analysis

concepts.name: Hilbert Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: An infinite-dimensional space that still has angles and perpendicularity, so geometry's intuition — projections, Pythagoras — works on functions.
concepts.formal_gloss: A complete inner product space; every element expands uniquely against an orthonormal basis, generalizing Euclidean geometry to infinitely many dimensions.

resources.title: MIT OCW 18.102 Introduction to Functional Analysis

sources: Mathematical foundation of quantum mechanics — high leverage

concepts.name: Fourier Series & Transform
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any signal, however jagged, is secretly a sum of pure sine waves — Fourier analysis is the prism that splits it into them.
concepts.formal_gloss: Decomposition of functions into orthogonal trigonometric (or complex exponential) components: series Σ c_n e^{inx} on periodic domains, and the integral transform f̂(ξ) = ∫ f(x)e^{-2πixξ}dx on R.

resources.title: Stein & Shakarchi, Fourier Analysis: An Introduction

concepts.name: Holomorphic Function
concepts.intuition_gloss: A function of a complex variable that is differentiable in every direction at once — a condition so rigid it forces the function to be infinitely smooth and locally a power series.
concepts.formal_gloss: A function f: C → C complex-differentiable on an open set, equivalently satisfying the Cauchy–Riemann equations; holomorphic implies analytic.

concepts.name: Cauchy's Integral Theorem & Residues
concepts.intuition_gloss: Integrating a well-behaved complex function around a closed loop gives zero — unless the loop encircles singularities, whose 'residues' alone determine the answer.
concepts.formal_gloss: ∮ f dz = 0 for f holomorphic on a simply connected domain; more generally the integral equals 2πi times the sum of residues at enclosed poles, weighted by winding numbers.
sources: Powers analytic number theory — high leverage
